name: Portugal
summary: A genuine study-and-work base with real tech hubs, a large Bangladeshi/Muslim community and an A2-only language bar, but the 2025-26 reforms gutted its headline edge: citizenship is now 10 years (clock from permit issuance), no long-term D visa is processed in Dhaka, and politics has lurched toward immigration tightening.

links.title: AIMA - Agency for Integration, Migration and Asylum
Study in Portugal (DGES)

job-market.summary: Real Lisbon/Porto tech hubs, English-friendly, both engineers placeable - but pay lags NW Europe (avg ~€59k Lisbon).
job-market.reasoning: Lisbon and Porto host Google, Microsoft, Cloudflare, Farfetch and VW Digital plus an English-speaking startup scene, so both software engineers have realistic prospects and AI/dev demand is rising. But the average Lisbon software-engineer salary is ~€58,954 gross (entry ~€41k, senior ~€66k) - well below Northern Europe - and the pool is competitive, so it is decent rather than best-in-class for this couple.
evidence: Lisbon average software-engineer salary ~€58,954 gross; entry ~€41,162, senior ~€66,353 (Glassdoor/SalaryExpert 2025-26).
International offices: Google, Microsoft, Cloudflare, Farfetch, VW Digital Solutions in Lisbon/Porto.
Senior/staff total comp at multinationals can reach €90k-€140k+ with equity.
English widely used in tech firms; salaries still materially lag NW Europe.

links.title: Glassdoor - Software Engineer salary Portugal
SalaryExpert - Software Engineer Lisbon 2026

visa-access.summary: No national D visa in Dhaka; BD applicants must apply via Embassy in New Delhi AND first hold an Indian double-entry visa.
visa-access.reasoning: Bangladeshi nationals cannot lodge a national (D / study / work / family) visa in Dhaka - those applications route to the Embassy of Portugal in New Delhi via VFS, and applicants must first obtain a double-entry X visa from the High Commission of India in Dhaka just to travel there. That double cross-border burden, plus document scrutiny and AIMA's onward backlog, makes the Dhaka experience a severe barrier despite Portugal's general visa reputation.
evidence: Long-term D visas (study €90, work €90, family €90) for BD residents are decided at the Embassy of Portugal in New Delhi via VFS.
BD applicants need a double-entry X visa from the Indian High Commission in Dhaka (max 3 months, ≤15-day stay) to apply in New Delhi.
Schengen short-stay for BD routes via the Embassy of France in Dhaka, not New Delhi.
Onward AIMA processing of standard permits runs ~3-6 months after biometrics.

links.title: Embassy of Portugal in New Delhi - Visas (jurisdiction incl. Bangladesh)
AIMA - residence and visa information

citizenship.summary: 2025-26 reform extended naturalisation from 5 to 10 years (BD is non-CPLP), clock from permit issuance. Dual allowed, A2 + civic test.
citizenship.reasoning: Portugal's famed 5-year route is gone: parliament approved the reform on 28 Oct 2025 (resubmitted and re-approved Apr 2026, promulgated by the President in May 2026), setting 10 years for non-EU/non-CPLP nationals - and Bangladesh is not a CPLP country, so the 7-year track does not apply. The residence clock now counts only from the date the permit is issued (reversing the 2024 amendment), pushing real timelines past a decade. Dual citizenship is still permitted (no renunciation of Bangladeshi nationality) and the bar is only A2 plus a civics test, which keeps this above the worst cases.
evidence: Non-CPLP third-country nationals (incl. Bangladesh): 10 years; CPLP/EU: 7 years.
Parliament approved 28 Oct 2025 (157-64); re-approved Apr 2026; promulgated by President May 2026.
Residence clock counts only from permit-issuance date, not application date.
Dual citizenship still allowed; requirements include A2 Portuguese, a civics test and a sworn democratic-principles declaration.

links.title: idealista - New Portuguese Nationality Law tightens citizenship
Global Citizen Solutions - Portuguese Nationality Law updates

post-study-work.summary: 12-month post-study job-seeker permit, full work rights, immediate spouse join - but non-renewable, so a job must be found fast.
post-study-work.reasoning: Graduates of Portuguese universities can obtain a 12-month post-study job-seeker residence permit with full work rights (subsistence proof ~€13,000, fee ~€93), and once employed the spouse and children can join immediately with their own permits. The window is non-renewable - employment must be secured within 12 months to convert to a work permit - so it is workable but shorter and less generous than the 3-year open permits in Canada/NZ.
evidence: Post-study job-seeker residence permit: 12 months, full work rights, ~€13,000 (1.5x IAS) subsistence, ~€93 fee.
Permit is non-renewable - must convert to a work permit within 12 months.
Once employed, spouse and children under 18 can join and receive permits without extra waiting.
Pathway then runs to PR in ~5 years and citizenship under the new 10-year rule.

links.title: Where to Emigrate - Portugal post-study job-seeker permit
Study in Portugal (DGES)

spouse-family.summary: Both-students entry gives each an independent work permit; skilled-worker/Blue Card sponsors skip the new 2-year reunification wait.
spouse-family.reasoning: For this two-engineer couple the cleanest route is both enrolling as students, so each holds an independent residence permit with study-period work rights - sidestepping the new Law 61/2025 rule that a third-country sponsor must hold a permit for 2 years (15 months if 18 months' prior cohabitation) before reuniting a spouse. Crucially, highly skilled worker and EU Blue Card sponsors are exempt from that waiting period, and reunified spouses may work on the same terms as the sponsor, so both can work across study and work phases.
evidence: Law 61/2025 (published 22 Oct 2025): general 2-year wait before family reunification, 15 months with 18-month prior cohabitation.
Highly skilled worker permits and EU Blue Card holders are exempt from the waiting period.
Reunified family members may live and work under the same conditions as the sponsor.
Both-students workaround: each spouse holds an independent permit with work rights from the start.

links.title: EU Immigration Portal - Family member in Portugal
AIMA - family reunification

msc-study.summary: 200+ English-taught masters, low non-EU tuition (~€3-7k/yr), part-time student work, spouse can enrol in parallel.
msc-study.reasoning: Portugal offers over 200 English-taught programmes including CS/data/AI masters at low non-EU tuition of roughly €3,000-7,000/yr, with monthly instalment options and part-time work rights during study. The spouse - also an engineer - can enrol in parallel so both hold independent permits, making the both-students entry the cleanest dual-work setup; the English-medium CS catalogue is still narrower than the UK or Netherlands.
evidence: 200+ English-taught programmes; non-EU masters tuition typically ~€3,000-7,000/yr.
Tuition payable in monthly instalments at most universities.
Students hold part-time work rights during studies.
Both spouses can enrol, each gaining an independent residence permit.

links.title: Mastersportal - Study a Master in Portugal
Study in Portugal (DGES)

pr-pathway.summary: Permanent residence after ~5 years legal stay + A2 Portuguese - far shorter than the 10-year citizenship bar, but AIMA timing is uncertain.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Permanent residence (and EU long-term resident status) is reachable after 5 years of legal residence with A2 Portuguese, stable income and tax/social-security compliance, and student and work years count - making PR notably more attainable than the new 10-year citizenship track. The drag is AIMA: although it cleared ~93% of inherited cases by Oct 2025, standard permits still run 3-6 months and card issuance 4-12 weeks, so timing remains less predictable than top-tier points systems.
evidence: PR after 5 years legal residence with A2 Portuguese, stable income and tax/social-security compliance.
Student and work years of legal residence count toward the 5-year threshold.
AIMA issued ~386,000 permits in 2025 and cleared ~93% of inherited backlog cases by Oct 2025.
Standard permits ~3-6 months; card issuance ~4-12 weeks post-approval.

links.title: AIMA - permanent residence
EU Immigration Portal - Portugal

income-cost.summary: Lower historic costs offset by surging Lisbon rents and tech pay below NW Europe - moderate net savings on two incomes.
income-cost.reasoning: A dual-engineer household lives comfortably above the Portuguese median, but Lisbon rents have surged (1-bed city-centre ~€1,424/mo; outside centre ~€1,070) while salaries lag Northern Europe, so savings are moderate rather than generous. The IFICI 20% flat tax meaningfully lifts take-home if both qualify, but on a single income Lisbon would be tight.
evidence: Lisbon 1-bed city-centre rent ~€1,424/mo; outside centre ~€1,070/mo (Numbeo, Jun 2026).
Couple basic monthly costs ex-rent roughly €1,300-1,500.
Tech pay sits well above the Portuguese median (~€32-40k) but below NW Europe.
Two tech incomes yield moderate savings; IFICI 20% flat tax can boost net pay.

links.title: Numbeo - Cost of Living in Lisbon
INE - Statistics Portugal

healthcare.summary: Universal tax-funded SNS, low cost and solid clinical quality, but GP shortages and long waits push many to private top-up.
healthcare.reasoning: Legal residents with a social-security number access the universal SNS at low or no cost and clinical quality in major hospitals is well regarded. But access is strained - 1,563,710 people lacked an assigned family doctor at end-2025, Lisbon and the Algarve are worst hit, and waiting lists are long - so affordable private insurance is commonly used in Lisbon/Porto.
evidence: Universal SNS covers all legal residents with a social-security number, low or no out-of-pocket cost.
1,563,710 people without an assigned family doctor at end-December 2025 (SNS transparency portal).
Lisbon, Algarve and Setúbal peninsula worst affected; waits can exceed a year for GP assignment.
Government 2026 order allows hiring up to 1,111 retired doctors to close the gap.

links.title: SNS - Serviço Nacional de Saúde
idealista - public healthcare in Portugal

culture-language.summary: High English use in cities/tech and universities; only A2 Portuguese for PR/citizenship, with a large South-Asian community easing settling.
culture-language.reasoning: English is widely spoken in Lisbon/Porto, tech workplaces and universities, so daily life and work are manageable without fluent Portuguese. The language bar for officialdom is modest - only A2 for PR and citizenship - and Portuguese is a relatively learnable Romance language, while the established Bangladeshi/South-Asian community provides a soft cultural landing.
evidence: High English proficiency in cities, tech firms and universities.
Only A2 Portuguese required for PR and citizenship - a modest, achievable bar.
Large Bangladeshi/South-Asian community supports integration.
Portuguese (Romance language) comparatively approachable to A2 level.

links.title: Study in Portugal (DGES)
AIMA - integration and language

safety-law.summary: Among the world's safest (7th on 2025 Global Peace Index); solid rule of law (WJP 29th/143), low violent crime.
safety-law.reasoning: Portugal ranks 7th on the 2025 Global Peace Index (score 1.371, 5th in Europe) with low violent crime and high perceived safety - strong reassurance for a relocating couple. Rule of law is solid but not top-tier (WJP 2025 ~29th of 143, with a noted decline), and 2024-25 heavy-handed police operations in immigrant areas like Martim Moniz are a minor caveat on fair treatment of migrants.
evidence: 7th worldwide in the 2025 Global Peace Index (score 1.371; 5th in Europe).
WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: ~29th of 143, with a slight decline.
Low violent crime; petty theft in tourist zones is the main everyday concern.
Dec 2024 Martim Moniz police operation drew large anti-racism protests in Jan 2025.

links.title: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 - Global Insights
The Portugal News - Portugal 7th safest country (2025 GPI)

politics.summary: Stable institutions but volatile: 3 elections in 3 years, far-right Chega now 2nd, and 2025-26 immigration/nationality tightening.
politics.reasoning: Portugal's institutions are democratic and stable, but it held its third general election in three years in May 2025 and far-right Chega surged to ~58 seats, tying for second and becoming decisive. Chega's support was crucial to passing the 28 Oct 2025 nationality reform and the Law 61/2025 immigration curbs - so policy risk for migrants is now real and rising, not theoretical.
evidence: Third general election in three years (May 2025); recurrent minority governments.
Chega rose to ~58 seats, tying for second; its votes carried the nationality reform (157-64).
2025: citizenship extended to 10 years and family reunification tightened (Law 61/2025).
Immigration is now a central political flashpoint, raising future-policy uncertainty.

links.title: Jobbatical - Portugal 2025 elections impact on immigration
Portugal.com - Portugal passes new nationality law

tax.summary: High top IRS (48% + surcharges), but IFICI/NHR 2.0 gives a 20% flat rate to qualifying tech professionals for 10 years.
tax.reasoning: Standard income tax is high - progressive to 48% above ~€81k plus a 2.5-5% solidarity surcharge - which weighs on dual high earners. The IFICI regime (NHR 2.0) offers a 20% flat rate on eligible Portuguese professional income for up to 10 years to highly qualified workers (EQF Level 6+ degree, science/tech sectors) not tax-resident in the prior 5 years, materially softening the burden if both engineers qualify.
evidence: Top marginal IRS 48% above ~€81k, plus 2.5-5% solidarity surcharge for high incomes.
IFICI (NHR 2.0): 20% flat rate on eligible PT professional income for up to 10 years.
Eligibility: new tax resident (no PT residence in prior 5 yrs) + EQF Level 6+ degree in science/tech/R&D.
Original NHR closed to new applicants; income from blacklisted jurisdictions taxed at 35%.

links.title: Sovereign Group - Portugal IFICI tax incentive regime
Autoridade Tributária e Aduaneira (Portuguese Tax Authority)

muslim-diaspora.summary: Established Bangladeshi hub in Lisbon (Martim Moniz), mosques and halal everywhere - but 2024-25 police raids signal rising hostility.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Lisbon hosts a long-standing, visible Bangladeshi community centred on Martim Moniz and Rua do Benformoso, with the 'Bangla masjid', halal butchers, restaurants and remittance services - a major comfort factor for this Muslim couple. The national Muslim population is modest but the Bangladeshi/South-Asian presence is strong; however, the Dec 2024 Martim Moniz police operation and Chega's rise signal rising anti-immigrant sentiment, so the trend is less reassuring than the community size alone.
evidence: Bangladeshi community concentrated in Lisbon's Martim Moniz; 'Bangla masjid' on Rua do Benformoso.
Rua do Benformoso lined with Bangladeshi restaurants, halal meat and money-transfer shops.
Halal butchers standard for community restaurants in Mouraria/Martim Moniz.
Dec 2024 police operation in Martim Moniz triggered large Jan 2025 anti-racism marches - rising-hostility signal.
